topic10.title: Azure Logic Apps — Fundamentals
topic10.concepts:
Azure Logic Apps is a cloud-based integration platform-as-a-service (iPaaS) that enables you to automate workflows and integrate apps, data, services, and systems. At its core, a Logic App is a workflow definition — a JSON document that describes a series of triggers and actions executed in a specified order. The platform abstracts away infrastructure management and provides a visual designer alongside a code view that exposes the underlying Workflow Definition Language (WDL). Logic Apps is particularly well-suited to replacing BizTalk-style orchestrations because it natively supports message transformation, routing, protocol adapters, and long-running stateful processes.

The two hosting plans — Consumption and Standard — represent fundamentally different runtime models. Consumption (also called multi-tenant) is a true serverless model where you pay per execution and each workflow runs in a shared Azure-managed environment. It supports exactly one workflow per Logic App resource, scales automatically, and stores run history in Azure Storage behind the scenes. Standard, by contrast, runs on the Azure Functions extensibility model (the Logic Apps runtime v2) hosted on App Service infrastructure. A single Standard Logic App resource can host multiple workflows, supports VNET integration natively, allows stateful and stateless workflow types, and enables local development with VS Code. Standard is priced by the hour (vCPU + memory) regardless of executions, making it better for high-throughput or enterprise scenarios where isolation and networking control matter.

Triggers define when a workflow starts. HTTP triggers expose an HTTPS endpoint that accepts inbound requests — Logic Apps generates a URL with an SAS signature you call from an external system. Recurrence triggers fire on a schedule (every N minutes, cron-style). Service Bus triggers can poll a queue or topic subscription, or use a push model (peek-lock by default) to consume messages. Event Grid triggers react to system or custom events published to a topic. Blob triggers fire when content is added or modified in a storage container. Understanding the polling vs push distinction is critical for interview discussions — most managed connectors use polling under the hood even if they look push-based, whereas HTTP and Event Grid are genuinely push.

Actions are the steps executed after a trigger fires. Connectors wrap external services with pre-built authentication and schema handling. An HTTP action makes a raw REST call to any endpoint. Condition actions implement if/else branching on expressions. For-each and Until loops handle iteration. Scope actions group child actions together, which is the foundation of error handling — a scope in a failed state can be caught by a subsequent 'Run After' configured action. Built-in connectors (also called in-app connectors in Standard) run directly in the Logic Apps runtime process, offering lower latency and no per-call cost in Standard. Examples include HTTP, Request, Schedule, Service Bus built-in, Azure Functions built-in, and Inline Code. Managed connectors run through the shared connector infrastructure (Logic Apps connector gateway), are billed per call in Consumption, and cover hundreds of SaaS systems like Salesforce, SAP, and Office 365.

The Workflow Definition Language (WDL) is a JSON schema that describes every aspect of a workflow: the trigger, each action's type and inputs, run-after conditions, retry policies, and expressions. Expressions are evaluated at runtime using the @ syntax — for example @{body('Parse_JSON')?['orderId']} reads a field from a prior action's output. Functions available in expressions include string manipulation (concat, replace, toLower), collection operations (first, last, union, intersection), logical functions (if, equals, and, or), date/time (utcNow, addDays), encoding (base64, base64ToString), JSON handling (json, string, xpath), and workflow metadata (triggerBody, outputs, variables). The xpath() function is particularly relevant for BizTalk migration since BizTalk maps heavily use XPath. Understanding how to write and debug expressions in code view is a senior-level differentiator.

terms.definition: Multi-tenant, serverless Logic Apps hosting. One workflow per resource. Pay per action execution. No VNET integration. Managed by Azure.
Single-tenant Logic Apps hosting on Azure Functions v2 runtime. Multiple workflows per resource. Hourly pricing. Native VNET integration. Supports stateful and stateless workflows.
The JSON-based schema that defines a Logic App workflow — triggers, actions, expressions, and run-after conditions.
The event or schedule that initiates a workflow run. Can be push-based (HTTP, Event Grid) or polling-based (Service Bus, Blob, Recurrence).
A single step within a workflow that performs work — calling an API, transforming data, branching logic, or looping.
A connector that runs in-process within the Logic Apps runtime. Lower latency, no per-call cost in Standard, available offline in local dev. Examples: HTTP, Service Bus (built-in), Azure Functions.
A connector hosted by the shared Azure connector infrastructure. Billed per call in Consumption. Covers hundreds of SaaS and enterprise systems.
A Standard-only workflow type that persists run history and intermediate state to Azure Storage, enabling long-running and resumable processes.
A Standard-only workflow type that runs entirely in memory with no persistent state. Faster and cheaper but run history is not retained by default.
A runtime-evaluated string in WDL using @ syntax. Accesses outputs from prior steps, applies functions, and computes dynamic values.
A condition on every action defining which preceding actions must complete — and in what state (Succeeded, Failed, Skipped, TimedOut) — before it runs. Enables try/catch patterns.
A container action that groups child actions. Used with Run After to implement try/catch error handling blocks.
The default Service Bus message consumption pattern in Logic Apps. The message is locked (not deleted) until the workflow completes successfully, then settled.
The callback URL generated for an HTTP trigger. Contains a signature parameter that authenticates incoming callers without requiring Azure AD.
The shared infrastructure that hosts managed connectors and brokers calls between Logic Apps and external systems.

  {
    "definition": {
      "$schema": "...",
      "triggers": { trigger-name: { type, inputs, recurrence } },
      "actions": {
        action-name: {
          type,
          inputs,
          runAfter: { prior-action: ["Succeeded"] }
        }
      },
      "outputs": {}
    }
  }

interview_qa.q: What are the key differences between Consumption and Standard Logic Apps, and when would you choose one over the other?
interview_qa.a: Consumption is multi-tenant, serverless, and supports one workflow per resource. It's ideal for low-to-medium volume scenarios, simple integrations, and cases where you want zero infrastructure management and pay-per-use pricing. Standard runs on the Azure Functions v2 runtime, supports multiple workflows in a single resource, includes native VNET integration, supports both stateful and stateless workflow types, and enables local development with VS Code. I choose Standard when I need private networking — for example, connecting to an on-premises system via ExpressRoute or a private endpoint — when I'm migrating BizTalk orchestrations that have multiple interrelated workflows, or when I need the cost predictability of hourly pricing at scale. In our BizTalk migration at EY, we standardized on Standard because our workflows needed to reach databases in a private VNET, and we needed the multi-workflow capability to mirror BizTalk's orchestration/pipeline separation.

interview_qa.q: Explain the difference between built-in connectors and managed connectors. Why does it matter in Standard?
interview_qa.a: Built-in connectors, also called in-app connectors in Standard, run directly within the Logic Apps runtime process. This means they don't make an extra network hop through the shared connector gateway, they have lower latency, and in Standard they carry no per-call charge. Examples include HTTP, the Request trigger, Service Bus built-in, Azure Functions built-in, and Inline Code. Managed connectors, by contrast, are hosted by Azure's shared connector infrastructure and cover hundreds of SaaS systems. In Standard, the built-in Service Bus connector is significant because it supports session-based processing and in-order delivery natively, which the managed connector doesn't. When I need to ensure ordered processing of BizTalk-style sequential convoys, I always use the built-in Service Bus connector with sessions enabled rather than the managed one.

interview_qa.q: How does the HTTP trigger work, and how do you secure it?
interview_qa.a: The HTTP trigger exposes an HTTPS endpoint with a SAS-signed URL that Azure generates. The URL contains a sig parameter — an HMAC-SHA256 signature — that Logic Apps validates on every inbound request. The URL itself is the secret. For additional security you can restrict access using Azure's IP filtering on the Logic App, use the 'Shared Access Signature' setting to require callers to pass the full signed URL, or you can put APIM in front and validate a JWT or subscription key at the gateway before the request ever reaches Logic Apps. In practice for enterprise scenarios I always front the HTTP trigger with APIM so the Logic App URL is never exposed to external callers. You can also enable caller IP restriction in the Logic App's access control settings.

interview_qa.q: Walk me through how expressions work in Workflow Definition Language.
interview_qa.a: Expressions in WDL are enclosed in @{} when embedded in a string, or start with @ when they are the entire value. They're evaluated at runtime against the workflow's execution context. The most common patterns are accessing prior action outputs with body('ActionName')?['fieldName'], accessing trigger data with triggerBody()?['field'], using string functions like concat('Hello ', body('Step1')?['name']), date functions like utcNow('yyyy-MM-dd'), and conditional expressions like if(equals(variables('status'), 'active'), 'yes', 'no'). The ?['field'] syntax is the null-safe property accessor — it returns null rather than throwing if the property doesn't exist, which is critical for resilient workflows. For XML processing, xpath(xml(body('action')), '/root/element/text()') extracts values using XPath, which was essential when we migrated BizTalk maps that were XPath-heavy.

interview_qa.q: What is the difference between stateful and stateless workflows in Standard, and when would you use each?
interview_qa.a: Stateful workflows persist their run state and history to Azure Storage after every action. This enables long-running workflows, workflow suspension and resumption, re-running failed workflows from the portal, and full run history inspection. The tradeoff is latency and cost — every action involves a round-trip to Storage. Stateless workflows run entirely in memory with no persistence. They're faster, cheaper, and suited for simple synchronous request/response patterns — essentially a replacement for a Function App when you want the visual designer. The limitation is that run history isn't retained by default (you can enable it for debugging), and the workflow must complete in a single execution without suspension. In our BizTalk migration, the main orchestrations became stateful workflows because they involved multiple async steps with human approval waits, while simple message routing and transformation pipelines became stateless for performance.

interview_qa.q: How does the Service Bus trigger work in Logic Apps, and what is peek-lock?
interview_qa.a: Logic Apps uses peek-lock as its default message consumption mode for Service Bus. When the trigger fires, it locks the message on the queue without deleting it. The message remains locked for the duration of the workflow (up to the lock duration configured on the queue, which can be renewed automatically). If the workflow completes successfully, Logic Apps sends a Complete settlement to remove the message. If the workflow fails, Logic Apps sends an Abandon settlement, returning the message to the queue for retry. If max delivery count is exceeded, the message moves to the dead-letter queue. This is critical for exactly-once processing guarantees. In my projects, I always configure dead-letter queue monitoring with alerts because a spike in DLQ messages is the first signal of a processing failure.

interview_qa.q: What are 'Run After' conditions and how do they enable try/catch patterns?
interview_qa.a: Every action in WDL has a runAfter property that specifies which preceding actions it depends on and in what terminal states — Succeeded, Failed, Skipped, or TimedOut. By default, an action runs only after its predecessor Succeeds. A try/catch pattern uses a Scope action to wrap the 'try' block. A subsequent 'catch' action is then configured with runAfter: { TryScope: ['Failed', 'TimedOut'] } so it executes only when the Scope fails. Inside the catch, you can extract the error details using result('TryScope') which returns the outputs including the error message and tracking ID. I always put the catch block inside a separate Scope too, so errors in error handling itself are contained. This is the closest equivalent to BizTalk's exception handling orchestrations.

interview_qa.q: How do you handle large messages in Logic Apps?
interview_qa.a: Logic Apps has a message size limit — 100MB for actions in Standard, less in some connectors. For large payloads, chunking is the answer. HTTP-based connectors support content chunking automatically when you enable 'Allow chunking' in the action settings — the runtime splits the payload into smaller chunks and reassembles on the receiving end using the Range header pattern. For blob-based large file processing, the pattern is to pass a blob reference (SAS URL or path) between steps rather than the blob content itself — the workflow operates on the reference, and each step reads/writes the blob directly. This is the equivalent of BizTalk's large message handling via the streaming database. In XSLT transformations of large XML, I pre-process by splitting via XPath into sub-documents, transform each, then aggregate.

interview_qa.q: What is the Workflow Definition Language $schema and what are the main top-level sections?
interview_qa.a: The WDL $schema points to the JSON schema definition for Logic Apps, currently https://schema.management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Logic/schemas/2016-06-01/workflowdefinition.json. The top-level sections are: triggers (exactly one trigger per workflow, though it can be a batch trigger aggregating multiple), actions (a flat dictionary of all action objects — nesting is expressed through runAfter, not JSON nesting), parameters (workflow-level parameters referenced with @parameters('name')), variables (initialized in Initialize Variable actions, mutated with Set Variable), and outputs (values the workflow exposes to callers). One common interview mistake is thinking actions are nested in JSON — they're flat with dependencies expressed through runAfter. The designer renders them as a visual flow, but the underlying JSON is a flat map.

interview_qa.q: How do you use the designer versus code view, and what are the limitations of each?
interview_qa.a: The designer is a visual canvas that renders the workflow as a flowchart. It's excellent for building quickly, configuring connectors with guided authentication, and understanding flow at a glance. Its limitations are that some advanced WDL features aren't exposed in the designer — for example, configuring specific retry policies, setting operationOptions flags, writing complex expressions, or configuring chunking. Code view exposes the raw JSON workflow definition. I switch to code view when I need to copy-paste action blocks between workflows, set advanced properties the designer hides, debug expression errors by reading the exact JSON, or when reviewing changes in source control pull requests. In our migration project I maintained code view as the source of truth and version-controlled the workflow JSON, using the designer only for initial scaffolding and for demo purposes with stakeholders.

talking_points: When I was leading the BizTalk migration at EY, one of the first decisions we made was Consumption vs Standard. We had 40+ workflows to migrate, several of which needed to reach SQL databases in a private VNET. Consumption was off the table immediately — it has no VNET integration. We went Standard, which also meant we could bundle related workflows into a single resource and reduce our ARM template sprawl.

I've had to explain the built-in vs managed connector distinction to my team multiple times. The concrete moment it mattered was with Service Bus — the managed connector polls every 30 seconds by default and doesn't support sessions. The built-in connector in Standard supports session-based processing, which we needed for in-order message processing that mirrored our BizTalk sequential convoy patterns.

Expression debugging is where juniors struggle. I developed a habit of always testing expressions in the 'Test Expression' panel in the designer before putting them into actions. The null-safe ?[] accessor saved us from dozens of NullReference-equivalent failures in production — BizTalk was more forgiving with missing fields, Logic Apps expressions will throw if you use [] instead of ?[].

The stateful vs stateless decision in Standard is architectural. Our approval workflows — which could sit waiting for a human response for 3 days — had to be stateful. Our real-time transformation pipelines that needed sub-second response times became stateless. I think of it as: if the workflow can fail and be re-run from the portal, it's stateful. If it's fire-and-forget transformation, it's stateless.

I always put APIM in front of Logic App HTTP triggers. The generated SAS URL is essentially an unrevocable credential — if it leaks, you have to regenerate it and update every caller. With APIM in front, the Logic App URL is a backend secret, and I control authentication at the gateway layer with subscription keys or JWT validation.

topic11.title: Azure Logic Apps — Runtime & Patterns
topic11.concepts:
Logic Apps run history is the primary operational interface for monitoring and debugging. Every workflow execution produces a run record containing the trigger inputs, each action's inputs and outputs, duration, and terminal status. In Consumption, run history is retained for 90 days. In Standard, retention is configurable. You can re-run a failed or cancelled run directly from the portal, which replays the original trigger payload — useful for transient failures like network timeouts. Re-run does not consume a new trigger event from Service Bus; it replays the stored trigger data. Retry policies are configured per action: Fixed Interval, Exponential Interval, or None. The default is 4 retries with 20-second exponential backoff. For Service Bus message processing, retry policy works in conjunction with peek-lock renewal — the action retries within the same locked message window.

Error handling in Logic Apps uses the Scope + Run After pattern, which is the closest equivalent to try/catch/finally. You wrap a group of actions in a Scope action. If any child action fails, the Scope transitions to Failed state. A subsequent action configured with runAfter: { ScopeName: ['Failed', 'TimedOut'] } acts as the catch block. Inside the catch, the result() function — for example result('Try_Scope') — returns an array of all actions that ran within the scope, including their status and error details. A finally pattern is achieved by configuring an action to run after the scope on both Succeeded AND Failed states. For compensation logic (BizTalk-style undo), you implement a catch block that calls rollback endpoints or sends compensating messages.

Parallel branches enable concurrent action execution within a workflow. In the designer, you add parallel branches from any action. In WDL, parallel execution is implicit — two actions that share the same runAfter predecessor with no dependency between them are automatically executed in parallel by the runtime. The runtime waits for all parallel branches to complete before proceeding to actions that depend on them. Until loops are used for polling patterns — repeatedly calling an endpoint until a condition is met, with configurable iteration count and timeout. The combination of parallel branches and loops is used for fan-out/fan-in patterns: split a payload into items, process each in parallel, then aggregate results.

Integration Accounts are a separate Azure resource that provides enterprise messaging capabilities: XSLT maps, XML schemas (XSD), trading partner agreements (AS2, EDIFACT, X12), certificates, and assemblies. An Integration Account is linked to a Logic App (Consumption or Standard). Maps enable XSLT-based XML transformation — directly analogous to BizTalk maps. The Transform XML action applies an XSLT map from the linked Integration Account to a payload. Schemas are used for XML validation. AS2, EDIFACT, and X12 connectors implement EDI encode/decode, using partner profiles and agreements stored in the Integration Account. This is the primary migration path for BizTalk EDI solutions.

Logic Apps Standard supports running multiple workflows within one resource, which enables child workflow patterns. One workflow can call another using the workflow built-in connector, passing inputs and receiving outputs. This mirrors BizTalk's called orchestrations. For monitoring, integrating Logic Apps with Application Insights provides distributed tracing, custom properties on tracked events (correlation IDs, business identifiers like order numbers), and log-based alerting. In Standard, Application Insights integration is configured at the Logic App resource level and automatically captures all workflow telemetry. Custom tracking schemas let you emit structured events at specific actions using the tracked properties configuration, which is the equivalent of BizTalk's BAM (Business Activity Monitoring).

terms.definition: The record of every workflow execution, including trigger data, each action's inputs/outputs, timestamps, and status. Accessible in the portal and queryable via the Logic Apps REST API.
Replaying a previous workflow execution using the stored trigger payload. Does not re-consume the original trigger event source (e.g., Service Bus message).
Per-action configuration defining how many times and at what interval an action is retried on failure. Types: Fixed, Exponential, None.
A container that groups child actions. Used with runAfter conditions to implement try/catch/finally error handling patterns.
Returns the execution results of all actions within a named Scope, including status and error details. Used in catch blocks.
The dependency declaration on every action specifying which predecessor actions must complete and in which terminal states before this action executes.
Concurrent action execution. Implicit in WDL — actions with the same runAfter and no mutual dependency run simultaneously.
A loop that repeats until a boolean condition is met, with configurable maximum iteration count and timeout.
Iterates over an array, executing a set of actions for each item. Supports configurable concurrency (parallelism) degree.
An Azure resource storing enterprise integration artifacts: XSLT maps, XSD schemas, EDI partner agreements, certificates. Linked to a Logic App to enable Transform XML, Validate XML, and EDI actions.
Applies an XSLT map from a linked Integration Account to transform an XML input. Direct equivalent of BizTalk Map.
EDI protocol connectors in Logic Apps. Require an Integration Account with configured partners and agreements. Migrate BizTalk EDI send/receive ports.
A Logic Apps Standard workflow called by another workflow using the built-in Workflow connector. Enables modular, reusable orchestration logic.
Custom metadata added to Application Insights telemetry events at specific actions, enabling business-level search and correlation (order ID, customer ID, etc.).
Automatic splitting of large message payloads into smaller chunks for HTTP-based connector actions that have message size limits.
A user-defined connector built from an OpenAPI/Swagger definition, available in Consumption or Standard, enabling standardized access to internal APIs.

interview_qa.q: How do you implement try/catch/finally error handling in Logic Apps?
interview_qa.a: I use the Scope + Run After pattern. I wrap the main logic in a Scope action called 'Try_Block'. The catch is a second Scope configured with runAfter: { Try_Block: ['Failed', 'TimedOut'] }. Inside the catch, I use result('Try_Block') to get the detailed error — it returns an array of action results with status, error code, and message. For a finally block — cleanup that must run regardless of success or failure — I add an action after both scopes with runAfter configured on both the Try_Block [Succeeded] and the Catch_Block [Succeeded, Failed]. In production I've extended this to include dead-letter logic in the catch: if the workflow was triggered by a Service Bus message and fails after exhausting retries, the catch block copies the message to a dead-letter table in SQL with the full error context, which is better than relying on the Service Bus DLQ alone because it preserves the workflow correlation ID alongside the failed message.

interview_qa.q: Describe the BizTalk orchestration to Logic Apps migration pattern. What maps to what?
interview_qa.a: The conceptual mapping is: BizTalk Orchestration → Logic Apps Stateful Workflow. BizTalk Receive Port/Pipeline → Trigger plus any decode/validate actions. BizTalk Send Port → The final action (HTTP call, Service Bus publish). BizTalk Map (.btm file) → XSLT map uploaded to Integration Account, applied via Transform XML action. BizTalk Schema (.xsd) → XSD schema in Integration Account, applied via Validate XML action. The MessageBox publish/subscribe → Service Bus topics with subscriptions. Called orchestrations → Child workflows via the Workflow built-in connector. Correlation Sets → Service Bus sessions (for session-based correlation) or a workflow variable matched against incoming data. The hardest parts of migration are BizTalk's parallel convoy patterns — which require session-based Service Bus processing in Logic Apps — and custom pipeline components, which become Azure Functions called inline. BAM reports are replaced by Application Insights tracked properties with a Power BI dashboard on top.

interview_qa.q: How do you monitor Logic Apps workflows in production, and how do you add business-level tracking?
interview_qa.a: I always integrate Logic Apps Standard with Application Insights at the resource level — it captures all run telemetry automatically. For business-level tracking, I configure Tracked Properties on key actions. For example, on the trigger action I add a tracked property 'orderId' mapped to triggerBody()?['orderId']. This emits that property on every Application Insights event for that run, enabling queries like: select all runs where orderId = 'ORD-12345'. In Log Analytics I build queries using customDimensions to find all runs for a given order across multiple workflows. I also emit custom events at business milestones — 'OrderReceived', 'TransformationComplete', 'SentToSAP' — by calling an HTTP action to the App Insights ingestion endpoint. Alert rules fire when error rate exceeds a threshold or when DLQ message count spikes, which I connect to PagerDuty for on-call. This is our equivalent of BizTalk's BAM portal.

interview_qa.q: What are the options for handling large messages in Logic Apps, and how did you approach this in a real project?
interview_qa.a: Logic Apps has message size limits: 100MB max for Standard, lower for many managed connectors. The primary strategies are chunking and blob offload. For HTTP connector actions, enabling chunking in settings tells the runtime to use HTTP Range headers to split the payload into configurable chunk sizes (default 50MB) and reassemble on the other side — both endpoints must support Range headers. For very large files, the recommended pattern is to never move the payload through the workflow at all. Instead, the trigger receives a reference — a blob SAS URL or a storage path — and each subsequent action reads from and writes to blob storage directly. In our EY migration, we had BizTalk pipelines processing flat files up to 200MB from SFTP. We replaced this with an AzCopy step that staged the file in blob storage, then a Logic App triggered by blob creation that processed the file using a streaming Azure Function rather than pulling the content into the workflow itself.

interview_qa.q: How do parallel branches work in Logic Apps and what are the concurrency controls?
interview_qa.a: Parallel execution in WDL is implicit — any two actions that have the same runAfter predecessor and no dependency on each other are scheduled to run in parallel by the runtime. In the designer this looks like a fork. The runtime waits for all parallel branches to complete before executing any action that depends on all of them. For For-Each loops, concurrency is configurable — the 'Degree of Parallelism' setting (runtimeConfiguration.concurrency.repetitions) controls how many items are processed simultaneously, from 1 (sequential) to 50. For actions that call downstream systems with rate limits, I set this to a low value like 5 to avoid overwhelming the target. For fan-out/fan-in patterns — process an array of items in parallel then aggregate — I use a For-Each with concurrency enabled, append results to a variable (though I avoid append-to-array in high-concurrency scenarios due to race conditions), or better yet use Durable Functions via an inline call when the fan-out is large.

interview_qa.q: How do you implement retry logic for Service Bus message processing and what happens when a message exceeds max delivery count?
interview_qa.a: Logic Apps handles Service Bus messages with peek-lock by default. If the workflow fails, the message is abandoned and returns to the queue. The Service Bus queue's max delivery count setting (default 10) controls how many attempts are made before the message is dead-lettered. Within Logic Apps, each action also has its own retry policy — up to 4 retries by default. So the interaction is: a single workflow run may retry an action 4 times internally; if the run still fails, the message is abandoned and a new run picks it up on the next poll. This repeats until the delivery count is exhausted. I configure monitoring on the dead-letter queue — a metric alert in Azure Monitor fires when DLQ message count exceeds 0. The DLQ processor is a separate Logic App workflow that reads dead-lettered messages, enriches them with the original error context, and routes them to an operations dashboard or triggers a human review workflow.

interview_qa.q: How do you migrate BizTalk EDI solutions to Logic Apps?
interview_qa.a: BizTalk's EDI capabilities — X12, EDIFACT, AS2 — map directly to Logic Apps Integration Account artifacts. You create an Integration Account and configure Trading Partners with their identifiers (ISA qualifier/ID for X12, GLN for EDIFACT). You define Agreements between partner pairs specifying encoding settings, acknowledgment requirements, and validation rules. The Logic Apps X12 Decode or EDIFACT Decode action then processes incoming EDI messages, splits them into individual transaction sets, validates against schemas stored in the Integration Account, and outputs structured XML. AS2 agreements handle the message signing, encryption, and MDN (acknowledgment) exchange. The output of decode is passed to Transform XML for mapping to your internal schema. The key difference from BizTalk: in Logic Apps you must explicitly handle the acknowledgment flow — in BizTalk the pipeline returned a 997/CONTRL ack automatically; in Logic Apps you implement that as an explicit Send action after decode.

interview_qa.q: What is a custom connector and when would you build one in Standard vs use a managed connector?
interview_qa.a: A custom connector in Logic Apps Standard is built from an OpenAPI 2.0 or 3.0 definition and deployed alongside your workflows in the same resource. It wraps an internal API with reusable authentication, schema documentation, and designer integration — your team sees it like a first-class connector in the designer. I build custom connectors when I have an internal API used across multiple workflows — for example, our enterprise logging service or the SAP integration middleware layer — to avoid copy-pasting HTTP action configurations. In Consumption, custom connectors are registered in Azure and available across all Logic Apps in the subscription. In Standard, custom connectors are workflow-scoped or can be shared via a custom NuGet package in advanced scenarios. Managed connectors — the pre-built ones from Microsoft — cover third-party SaaS and I never build custom connectors when a managed one exists, because Microsoft maintains the auth and schema updates.

interview_qa.q: How do you version-control and deploy Logic Apps workflows in a CI/CD pipeline?
interview_qa.a: For Standard, the workflow JSON lives in the file system of the Logic App (similar to Functions) and is part of the App Service deployment. I keep all workflow.json files and connections.json in a Git repository. The ARM/Bicep template provisions the Logic App resource and app settings; the workflow content is deployed separately via zip deploy or az logicapp workflow upload. In GitHub Actions, the pipeline runs Bicep deployment for infrastructure, then deploys workflow artifacts. For environment promotion, I use Logic App parameters (parameters.json) to externalize environment-specific values — Service Bus namespaces, API URLs — and swap the parameters file per environment while keeping the workflow JSON identical. Connection references in Standard use managed identity rather than connection strings where possible, which eliminates environment-specific secrets from the workflow definition entirely. For Consumption, I use ARM template exports carefully, since the designer sometimes generates non-idempotent ARM JSON.

interview_qa.q: Compare Logic Apps run history re-run versus replaying a message from Service Bus DLQ — when do you use each?
interview_qa.a: Re-run from run history replays the exact stored trigger payload from a previous execution. It's appropriate for transient failures — a downstream API was temporarily unavailable, a network timeout occurred — where the original data is correct and you just need to retry the processing. It does not re-read from Service Bus; it uses the cached trigger data from the run record. Re-processing from the DLQ is appropriate when the message failed due to a processing logic issue — a bug that you've now fixed — and the original Service Bus message is in the dead-letter queue. In this case, you forward the message back to the main queue (using Service Bus Explorer, a portal action, or a dedicated re-processing Logic App), and the workflow picks it up fresh as a new message with a new run record. I never use run history re-run as a substitute for DLQ management — if there's a systemic issue, re-running from history doesn't clear the DLQ messages, and you end up with duplicate processing attempts.

talking_points: The scope/try-catch pattern was the first thing I had to teach every developer on our BizTalk migration team. In BizTalk, exception handling was a separate orchestration shape — it felt native. In Logic Apps, it's a pattern you have to deliberately construct. Once I standardized our 'error handling template' — a scope with a catch block that always sends to our alerting service and logs to Application Insights — our team stopped writing workflows without it.

Our BizTalk EDI migration was the most technically involved part of the project. We had 12 X12 trading partners, each with different ISA qualifiers and acknowledgment requirements. The Integration Account approach worked well — creating partner profiles and agreements is a one-time setup. The part BizTalk handled automatically that we had to build explicitly was the 997 acknowledgment flow. I built a reusable child workflow for that.

Large message handling bit us early in the migration. We had a BizTalk pipeline processing 150MB SFTP files inline. When we naively migrated that to Logic Apps, we hit the action size limit immediately. The solution was to never move the file content through the workflow — stage it in blob storage, pass the SAS URL, and use a streaming Azure Function for the actual transformation. The workflow became an orchestrator of references, not a processor of payloads.

For monitoring, I always present the tracked properties approach to stakeholders as our replacement for BAM. I show them: here's a Log Analytics query that gives you every workflow that processed order X, with start time, duration, and whether it succeeded. That kind of business-level visibility was what BAM provided, and Application Insights tracked properties replicate it. It takes about a day to set up the queries and pin them to a dashboard.

Our CI/CD pipeline for Logic Apps Standard uses GitHub Actions with two stages: infrastructure (Bicep) and application (zip deploy of workflow artifacts). The key insight was to use connection references with managed identity everywhere possible. When you use managed identity for Service Bus, your workflow.json has no credentials — it just references the managed identity connection. That means the same workflow JSON deploys to dev, staging, and prod without any modification.

topic12.title: Azure Functions
topic12.concepts:
Azure Functions is a serverless compute platform that executes event-driven code without requiring explicit server management. The programming model centers on a function — a unit of code bound to exactly one trigger and zero or more input/output bindings. The trigger determines what initiates the function: an HTTP request, a timer, a message arriving on a queue, an event in a stream, or a file in blob storage. Bindings eliminate boilerplate integration code by declaratively connecting the function to external services — an output binding to a Service Bus queue, for example, means your function simply returns a value and the runtime handles the queue publish, authentication, and serialization. This binding model is one of the biggest productivity advantages over writing the same integration code in a Web API or Logic App HTTP action.

Hosting plans define the execution environment, scaling behavior, and cost model. The Consumption plan is purely serverless — you pay per execution and per GB-second of memory, functions scale from zero to many instances automatically, and the tradeoff is cold start latency (typically 1-10 seconds for .NET, longer for Java). The Premium plan runs on pre-warmed instances, eliminates cold starts, supports VNET integration, allows unlimited execution duration, and enables larger instance sizes. The Dedicated (App Service) plan runs Functions on an App Service plan you manage — useful when you already have underutilized App Service capacity or need predictable performance. Flex Consumption, a newer plan (GA in 2024), combines per-execution billing with VNET support, fast scale-out using KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling), and configurable instance memory. It addresses the two main gaps in the original Consumption plan: VNET integration and cold starts.

Durable Functions extends the Azure Functions programming model to support long-running, stateful orchestrations directly in code. The Durable Functions extension uses Azure Storage (or Netherite/SQL backend) to persist orchestration state, enabling workflows that survive restarts, wait for external events, and run for days. The orchestrator function defines the workflow — it calls activity functions, waits on external events, or starts sub-orchestrations. Activity functions do the actual work (CPU-bound or I/O-bound tasks). Entity functions (also called Durable Entities) implement the actor model — stateful objects that can be called and queried. The fan-out/fan-in pattern in Durable Functions is idiomatic: the orchestrator starts N activity tasks in parallel using Task.WhenAll, waits for all to complete, then aggregates results — handling potentially thousands of parallel tasks with automatic retry and state persistence.

Scaling in Azure Functions is instance-based. The runtime adds more instances of the function host process in response to incoming load. In Consumption, the scale controller monitors queue depth, event hub lag, or request rate and adds instances (up to 200 for Consumption, unlimited for Premium). Each instance runs all functions in the Function App, not just the triggered one — this is why co-locating high-throughput and low-throughput functions in the same app can be problematic. In Flex Consumption, KEDA controls scaling with fine-grained per-function scaling targets. Cold start is caused by the time required to start a new function host process, load the runtime, and JIT-compile (for .NET). Mitigations include: Premium plan pre-warmed instances, the 'Always On' setting in Dedicated plans, keeping the function app warm with a timer trigger ping, using .NET Native AOT (ahead-of-time compilation), and reducing package size and startup dependencies.

Security for Functions operates at multiple layers. Function-level security uses function keys — shared secrets passed in the x-functions-key header or code query parameter. There are three key types: function keys (per-function), host keys (all functions in the app), and master keys (admin operations). For service-to-service authentication, managed identity is the preferred approach — the Function App's system-assigned identity is granted RBAC roles on downstream resources (Storage, Service Bus, Key Vault), and the SDK acquires tokens automatically using DefaultAzureCredential. VNET integration restricts inbound and outbound traffic: inbound private endpoints make the function accessible only from within the VNET; outbound VNET integration routes all egress through the VNET to reach private resources. For public APIs, putting APIM in front of Functions provides subscription key validation, JWT verification, rate limiting, and centralized policy enforcement.

terms.definition: The single event source that causes a function to execute. Each function has exactly one trigger. Examples: HttpTrigger, TimerTrigger, ServiceBusTrigger, EventHubTrigger, BlobTrigger, QueueTrigger, EventGridTrigger.
A declarative connection to an external resource. Input bindings provide data to the function; output bindings receive data from the function. Eliminates boilerplate auth and SDK calls.
Serverless, scale-to-zero hosting. Pay per execution. Cold start possible. No VNET integration (except preview). Max 200 instances.
Always-warm instances (no cold start). VNET integration. Larger instance sizes. Per-instance-hour pricing. Supports unlimited execution duration.
GA since 2024. Per-execution billing with VNET integration, KEDA-based scaling, configurable instance memory (2GB-4GB), and per-function scaling targets.
Functions run on an App Service Plan. Good for existing App Service capacity. 'Always On' setting prevents cold starts. Predictable cost.
The latency introduced when a new function host instance must be started from scratch: process creation, runtime loading, dependency initialization, and JIT compilation.
An extension that adds stateful orchestration to Azure Functions. Uses Azure Storage for state persistence. Enables long-running workflows, fan-out/fan-in, and external event waiting.
A Durable Functions function that defines the workflow logic. Must be deterministic (no I/O, no DateTime.Now). Calls activity functions, waits on events, and manages control flow.
A Durable Functions function that performs actual work (I/O, computation). Called by orchestrators. Supports automatic retry with configurable retry options.
A Durable Functions actor — a stateful object with operations that can be called and queried. Persists state across calls. Useful for counters, aggregators, and long-lived stateful objects.
A Durable Functions pattern: orchestrator starts N parallel activity tasks, waits for all with Task.WhenAll, then aggregates results.
An HMAC shared secret for authenticating HTTP trigger calls. Scope: function-level, host-level, or master (admin). Passed in x-functions-key header or 'code' query parameter.
Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaling. Used in Flex Consumption and Azure Container Apps to scale function instances based on external metrics (queue depth, event hub lag).
An Azure SDK credential type that tries multiple authentication methods in order (managed identity, environment, CLI, etc.). Enables code that works in both local dev and production without changes.
The preferred deployment method for Function Apps. A ZIP archive of the function app content is uploaded to the runtime, which extracts and runs it. Used in CI/CD pipelines.

topic12.diagram:
AZURE FUNCTIONS ARCHITECTURE

  Event Sources           Function App                  Outputs
  ─────────────           ─────────────────────         ──────────────
  HTTP Request ──────────► HttpTrigger function ───────► HTTP Response
  Timer (CRON) ──────────► TimerTrigger function ──────► SQL DB (output binding)
  Service Bus Q ─────────► ServiceBusTrigger ──────────► Service Bus Topic
  Event Hub ─────────────► EventHubTrigger ────────────► Event Hub (output)
  Blob created ──────────► BlobTrigger ────────────────► Blob (output binding)
  Queue message ─────────► QueueTrigger ───────────────► Queue (output binding)
  Event Grid ────────────► EventGridTrigger ───────────► Downstream HTTP

  BINDING EXAMPLE (C# — no boilerplate):

  [Function("ProcessOrder")]
  public async Task Run(
      [ServiceBusTrigger("orders", Connection = "SB")] OrderMessage msg,   // INPUT trigger
      [BlobInput("templates/{orderId}.xml", Connection = "Storage")]        // INPUT binding
          string template,
      [ServiceBusOutput("processed", Connection = "SB")]                    // OUTPUT binding
          IAsyncCollector<ProcessedOrder> output,
      FunctionContext ctx)
  {
      // No SDK calls for SB receive, blob read, SB publish — runtime handles all
  }

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  HOSTING PLAN COMPARISON

  Feature              Consumption    Premium       Flex Consump.   Dedicated
  ──────────────────   ──────────     ───────       ─────────────   ─────────
  Cold Start           Yes            No            Reduced         No (AlwaysOn)
  VNET Integration     No*            Yes           Yes             Yes
  Scale to Zero        Yes            No            Yes             No
  Pricing              Per-exec       Per-hour      Per-exec        Per-hour
  Max instances        200            Unlimited     Unlimited       Plan limit
  Exec timeout         10 min         Unlimited     Unlimited       Unlimited
  Scaling engine       Scale ctrl     Scale ctrl    KEDA            Manual/autoscale
  Local dev parity     Yes            Yes           Yes             Yes

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  DURABLE FUNCTIONS PATTERN — FAN-OUT / FAN-IN

  [HTTP Trigger]
       │
  [Orchestrator Function]
       │
       ├──► Activity: ProcessItem(item[0]) ──┐
       ├──► Activity: ProcessItem(item[1]) ──┤
       ├──► Activity: ProcessItem(item[2]) ──┤  Task.WhenAll()
       │         ...                          ┤
       └──► Activity: ProcessItem(item[N]) ──┘
                                              │
                                         [Aggregate Results]
                                              │
                                    [Return to Caller / Publish]

  State persisted to Azure Storage after every await — survives restarts

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  SECURITY LAYERS

  Internet
    │
  [APIM] — subscription key, JWT validate, rate limit, IP filter
    │
  [Function App] — private endpoint (inbound) / VNET integration (outbound)
    │           — function keys (host/function level)
    │           — managed identity (to downstream resources)
    │
  [Downstream: SB, Storage, Key Vault, SQL]
    └── RBAC granted to Function's managed identity

interview_qa.q: Explain the different Azure Functions hosting plans and when you'd choose each.
interview_qa.a: Consumption is true serverless — scale to zero, pay per execution, automatic scaling up to 200 instances. I use it for event-driven workloads with unpredictable or low traffic where cold start latency is acceptable — for example, a nightly batch job or a webhook handler that fires a few times per hour. Premium eliminates cold starts by keeping pre-warmed instances running at all times, supports VNET integration, and allows longer execution times. I use Premium when I need to connect to private resources (on-prem via ExpressRoute, private SQL) or when cold start latency is unacceptable for end-user-facing APIs. Flex Consumption is the newest plan — it gives Consumption-style per-execution pricing with VNET support and KEDA-based scaling. I'd use it for new projects that need VNET integration but want cost efficiency over Premium. Dedicated (App Service) I use when the team already manages App Service infrastructure and wants Functions to run alongside Web Apps on existing committed capacity.

interview_qa.q: What causes cold starts in Azure Functions and how do you mitigate them?
interview_qa.a: Cold start happens when the Functions runtime needs to start a new instance — it must initialize the host process, load the language worker, restore packages, initialize dependency injection containers, and JIT-compile code. For .NET, this is typically 1-3 seconds; for Java it can be 5-10 seconds. Mitigations in order of effectiveness: First, use Premium plan with pre-warmed instances — there's always at least one instance ready. Second, use Flex Consumption which has faster instance startup. Third, for .NET, compile with Native AOT which produces a self-contained binary with no JIT overhead. Fourth, minimize startup dependencies — avoid loading large config at startup, lazy-initialize expensive clients. Fifth, keep deployment packages small — tree-shake unused dependencies. In practice for our BizTalk-replacement Functions that were on the critical path for real-time order processing, we moved from Consumption to Premium specifically because a 3-second cold start on the first request of the day was visible to end users.

interview_qa.q: Explain Durable Functions — orchestrator, activity, entity — and the fan-out/fan-in pattern.
interview_qa.a: Durable Functions adds stateful orchestration to the Functions model. The orchestrator function defines workflow logic — it's a regular C# function decorated with [OrchestrationTrigger] that uses the IDurableOrchestrationContext API. The orchestrator must be deterministic because the Durable runtime replays it from the beginning every time it resumes from an await — so no DateTime.Now, no random, no direct I/O. Activity functions do the actual work and are called via context.CallActivityAsync — they can do any I/O, call APIs, query databases. The runtime handles retrying activities with configurable retry options. Entity functions implement the actor model — each entity has an ID and state, and you send operations to it via context.SignalEntityAsync. Fan-out/fan-in: the orchestrator creates a list of activity tasks (context.CallActivityAsync calls without awaiting each one), then awaits Task.WhenAll(tasks). The runtime executes all activities in parallel, persisting intermediate state to Storage after each completes. When all finish, Task.WhenAll returns and the orchestrator proceeds to the aggregation step. This pattern handles thousands of parallel tasks reliably.

interview_qa.q: How do bindings work and what are the advantages over writing SDK code directly?
interview_qa.a: Bindings are declarative metadata — in C# they're attributes on function parameters, in Python/Node they're entries in function.json. The Functions runtime reads the binding metadata, handles authentication (including managed identity token acquisition), creates the SDK client, and injects the typed object or value into your function parameter. For an output binding to Service Bus, you annotate a parameter with [ServiceBusOutput] and assign values to it — the runtime publishes those messages when the function completes. The advantages: first, authentication is automatic — with managed identity, no connection strings in code. Second, it eliminates repetitive boilerplate — no ServiceBusClient creation, no try/finally for proper disposal. Third, it enables batch processing naturally — output bindings can collect multiple messages and batch-publish them. The limitation is that bindings support only common patterns — for complex SDK operations like Service Bus sessions or transaction scopes, you inject the SDK client directly using dependency injection and write the code explicitly.

interview_qa.q: How do you secure an Azure Function that's called by APIM?
interview_qa.a: The pattern I use: APIM handles external authentication (subscription key, JWT validation) and forwards requests to the Function. The Function itself is secured with host-level function keys — APIM stores the function key as a Named Value (or Key Vault reference) and passes it in the x-functions-key header on every backend request. Additionally, I configure the Function App to restrict inbound calls to APIM's outbound IP addresses using IP restrictions in the Networking settings — so only APIM can reach the function endpoint directly. For VNET-integrated deployments, the Function App has no public endpoint and APIM reaches it via private DNS through the VNET. For the function's outbound calls to other services (Service Bus, Storage, SQL), I use managed identity with RBAC — no connection strings. The Function App system-assigned identity gets Service Bus Data Sender, Storage Blob Data Contributor, and Key Vault Secrets User roles as needed.

interview_qa.q: Describe a real scenario where you used an Azure Function triggered by Service Bus for data transformation.
interview_qa.a: In our BizTalk migration at EY, we had a BizTalk receive port that consumed flat-file EDIFACT messages from a Service Bus queue, transformed them to our internal XML schema, and published to a downstream queue. We replaced this with a .NET Azure Function with ServiceBusTrigger on the input queue and ServiceBusOutput binding on the output queue. The transformation logic — flat file parsing and XSLT — ran inside the function. We used the built-in Service Bus SDK for peek-lock so we could control settlement explicitly: if transformation succeeded, we called CompleteMessageAsync; if it failed due to a data issue (malformed EDIFACT), we called DeadLetterMessageAsync with the error reason; if it was a transient failure, we let the function throw and the runtime abandoned the message for retry. We instrumented with Application Insights custom events — 'TransformationStarted', 'TransformationCompleted', 'TransformationFailed' — with the message's correlation ID as a tracked property, enabling end-to-end tracing from inbound Service Bus message to outbound.

interview_qa.q: How does scaling work in Azure Functions, and what are the implications of co-locating multiple functions?
interview_qa.a: The Functions scale controller monitors trigger metrics — queue depth for Service Bus/Queue triggers, event hub consumer group lag, HTTP concurrency — and adds instances of the function host process. Each instance runs all functions in the Function App. This means if you have a high-throughput Service Bus function and a low-traffic HTTP function in the same app, scaling driven by the Service Bus queue will also create more instances of the HTTP function, consuming resources unnecessarily. Conversely, a single slow function can hold up an instance needed by other functions. My practice: separate high-throughput, business-critical functions into dedicated Function Apps with their own scaling policy. Group low-traffic, related utility functions together. In Flex Consumption, per-function scaling targets address this — you can set different scale targets per function even within the same app. Max instances per app should be set explicitly to avoid thundering herd scenarios when a queue backs up and 200 instances all try to connect to a database that supports 100 connections.

interview_qa.q: How do you deploy Function Apps in a CI/CD pipeline?
interview_qa.a: My standard pipeline in GitHub Actions: first stage runs Bicep to provision the Function App resource, app settings, managed identity assignments, and VNET integration. Second stage builds the function project (dotnet publish for .NET), packages the output into a ZIP, and deploys with az functionapp deployment source config-zip. I always use run-from-package mode (WEBSITE_RUN_FROM_PACKAGE=1 app setting) because it deploys atomically — the package is mounted as read-only and swapped instantly with no file extraction downtime. For blue/green deployments, I use deployment slots: deploy to the staging slot, run smoke tests, then az functionapp deployment slot swap. Slot swap is near-instantaneous and supports rollback by swapping back. App settings that should be slot-specific (the Service Bus connection for staging vs prod) are marked as 'slot settings' so they don't swap with the code. Secrets come from Key Vault references in app settings — the Function App's managed identity reads them at startup.

interview_qa.q: What is the difference between EventHub trigger and Service Bus trigger, and when do you use each?
interview_qa.a: Event Hub is designed for high-throughput telemetry and event streaming — millions of events per second, retained for 1-7 days (up to 90 with premium), and consumed by multiple consumer groups independently. The Event Hub trigger in Functions uses a consumer group to read events in order per partition. It's ideal for IoT telemetry, clickstream data, and scenarios where you need stream processing semantics. Service Bus is a message broker with enterprise messaging guarantees — at-most-once or at-least-once delivery, FIFO ordering within sessions, dead-lettering, duplicate detection, and message scheduling. The Service Bus trigger is ideal for command/task processing where each message must be processed exactly once by exactly one consumer, with explicit settlement control. I use Event Hub when the consumer is doing aggregation or analysis and missed events are acceptable; I use Service Bus when each message represents an action that must be completed reliably — like processing an order or calling a downstream API.

interview_qa.q: How do you handle poison messages in an Azure Function with Queue or Service Bus trigger?
interview_qa.a: For Service Bus: the peek-lock model means the Functions runtime locks the message during processing. If the function throws an exception, the runtime abandons the message, returning it to the queue. The delivery count increments. When delivery count exceeds the queue's max delivery count (default 10), Service Bus automatically moves the message to the dead-letter queue (DLQ). I explicitly handle the two failure categories differently: for data-quality failures (malformed message that will never succeed), I call DeadLetterMessageAsync immediately with a descriptive reason, rather than exhausting retries. For transient failures, I let the retry policy handle it. I always have a DLQ processor — either a separate function or a Logic App — that reads DLQ messages, enriches them with context, and routes them to an operations queue for manual review. For Azure Queue Storage triggers (not Service Bus), Functions automatically moves a message to a poison queue (named originalqueue-poison) after 5 failed attempts — I monitor that queue with an alert.

talking_points: The binding model was the biggest productivity gain when we moved from BizTalk custom pipeline components to Azure Functions. In BizTalk, a pipeline component reading from a database and writing to a queue required 200 lines of boilerplate. The equivalent Function with input and output bindings is 20 lines — the runtime handles auth, connection pooling, and settlement.

Cold start was a real production issue for us. Our BizTalk-replacement order processing function was on the critical path — the first order of the morning would take 4 seconds longer than all subsequent ones. That showed up in SLA reports. We moved to Premium, the pre-warmed instance eliminates that latency entirely, and the cost difference was justified by the SLA improvement.

For the Service Bus-triggered transformation function, the decision to call DeadLetterMessageAsync directly for data-quality failures rather than letting retries exhaust was important. If you let a malformed EDIFACT message retry 10 times at 30-second intervals, you've wasted 5 minutes and created noise in monitoring. Distinguishing transient vs permanent failures at code level and settling immediately was something I learned from painful early production incidents.

Managed identity for Function Apps was a security requirement at EY — no connection strings in app settings, everything through Key Vault references or MSI. The practical implication is that DefaultAzureCredential in the SDK works seamlessly: locally it picks up my developer Azure CLI credentials; in production it uses the managed identity. Zero code change between environments.

I introduced Durable Functions for our approval workflow scenario — a function that needed to pause for up to 72 hours waiting for a manager to approve an order above threshold. The alternative was a polling loop in a Logic App. Durable Functions handled this cleanly: orchestrator calls WaitForExternalEvent with a 72-hour timeout; the approval UI calls the Durable HTTP API to raise the event; if timeout expires, the orchestrator takes the auto-rejection path. No polling, no infrastructure cost while waiting.
