topic13.title: Azure API Management (APIM)
topic13.concepts:
Azure API Management is a fully managed API gateway platform that sits between API consumers and backend services. It provides a three-component architecture: the gateway (the runtime proxy that intercepts all API calls, applies policies, and routes to backends), the developer portal (a customizable website where API consumers discover, read documentation, test, and subscribe to APIs), and the management plane (the Azure control plane — ARM API, Azure portal, Azure CLI — through which you configure APIs, products, policies, and users). The gateway is the critical runtime component — every API call flows through it, and it executes policy documents that can inspect, transform, enrich, and block requests at multiple lifecycle points.

APIM's deployment mode determines network access. In External mode (the default), the APIM gateway has a public IP and is reachable from the internet. This is suitable for public-facing APIs. In Internal mode, the gateway is deployed inside a VNET with no public IP — it's accessible only from within the VNET or from connected networks via ExpressRoute or VPN. Internal mode is the standard choice for enterprise integration platforms where APIs are consumed by internal applications or partner networks. A common enterprise pattern puts an Application Gateway (with WAF, layer-7 load balancing, and TLS termination) in front of an Internal-mode APIM — the App Gateway has a public IP, WAF protects against OWASP threats, and it forwards clean traffic to the APIM private IP. APIM then routes to private backends. This gives you WAF protection, DDoS handling, and certificate offload at the App Gateway layer, plus API management, rate limiting, and JWT validation at the APIM layer.

Policies are the heart of APIM. A policy document is XML that contains four sections: inbound (executes before the request reaches the backend), backend (controls how APIM calls the backend, including retry and circuit breaker), outbound (executes after the backend responds, before the response reaches the caller), and on-error (executes when an exception occurs in any prior section). Policies compose — you can nest policy statements and control flow using conditions, choose-when blocks, and forward-request. Built-in variables like context.Request, context.Response, context.User, context.Subscription, and context.Variables provide access to request/response data and policy-level state. Named values (formerly 'properties') are key-value pairs — including Key Vault references — that externalize secrets from policy XML. This is critical for secrets rotation: the policy references a named value, the named value references a Key Vault secret, and rotating the secret in Key Vault is automatically picked up without policy changes.

The most important individual policies for senior-level discussion are: rate-limit-by-key (limit call rate per subscription, IP, or any key expression), validate-jwt (verify a JWT from Azure AD or any OIDC provider — validates signature, issuer, audience, and claims), set-header (add/overwrite/remove headers on request or response), rewrite-uri (transform the inbound URL before forwarding to the backend — strip API version prefixes, map paths), send-request (make an HTTP call to any external service from within the policy pipeline — used for token exchange, enrichment, and direct Service Bus publishing), and authentication-managed-identity (acquire a managed identity token for the target resource and set it as the Authorization header for the backend call). The combination of send-request and authentication-managed-identity enables APIM to publish directly to Service Bus or call Azure services without exposing credentials.

APIM's product and subscription model is the access control layer for API consumers. A Product groups one or more APIs and defines access terms — whether approval is required, call quotas, and rate limits. Subscriptions are granted to products and carry the subscription key that callers pass in the Ocp-Apim-Subscription-Key header. This enables multi-tenant API access where different consumers have different rate limits and API access sets. Application Insights integration for monitoring: you connect an Application Insights instance to APIM in the portal and configure which APIs and operations emit telemetry. APIM emits request/response events as Application Insights dependencies, and you add custom dimensions (like the subscription ID or product name) using the policy-level emit-metric or set-variable statements to create dashboards showing API usage by consumer.

terms.definition: The APIM runtime component that processes every API call — applying policies, authenticating, rate limiting, and routing to backends.
A customizable web portal where API consumers discover APIs, read OpenAPI documentation, test calls interactively, and manage their subscriptions.
The Azure control plane for configuring APIM — ARM API, Azure portal, Azure CLI, Terraform. Manages APIs, operations, policies, products, and users.
APIM deployed inside a VNET with no public IP. Accessible only from within the VNET or connected networks. Standard for enterprise deployments.
APIM with a public IP accessible from the internet, while still optionally joined to a VNET for backend connectivity.
An XML document attached to an API, operation, product, or globally that defines inbound/backend/outbound/on-error policy execution logic.
Policy section that executes when a request arrives at APIM — before it's forwarded to the backend. Used for authentication, validation, and transformation.
Policy section controlling how APIM calls the backend — retry, timeout, circuit breaker, and backend URL.
Policy section executing after the backend responds — before the response is sent to the caller. Used for response transformation and header stripping.
Policy section executing when any exception occurs. Used for custom error responses and error logging.
A key-value store in APIM for externalizing constants and secrets from policy XML. Supports plain text, secret (encrypted), and Key Vault reference types.
A grouping of one or more APIs with defined access terms (approval, quotas, rate limits). Consumers subscribe to products, not APIs directly.
A 32-character key generated per subscription, passed by callers in the Ocp-Apim-Subscription-Key header (or query param). The primary consumer authentication mechanism.
An APIM policy that validates a JWT — checking signature (using OIDC discovery or explicit keys), issuer, audience, expiry, and specific claim values.
Makes an HTTP call to any URL from within the APIM policy pipeline. Used for token exchange, backend enrichment, and direct Azure service calls (Service Bus, Event Grid).
Acquires an OAuth2 token for APIM's managed identity targeting a specified resource. Sets the token as the Authorization Bearer header for the backend call.
An APIM resource that encapsulates a backend service URL and its authentication configuration. Enables centralized backend management and circuit breaker configuration.
Azure's layer-7 load balancer with Web Application Firewall. Placed in front of Internal APIM to provide public ingress, WAF protection, and TLS termination.

topic13.diagram:
APIM ENTERPRISE ARCHITECTURE

  Internet
     │
  [Application Gateway + WAF (public IP)]
  - OWASP rule set, DDoS protection
  - TLS termination, certificate management
  - Layer-7 routing (path-based)
     │  (private IP, VNET-internal)
     ▼
  [APIM — Internal Mode]
  - Developer Portal (internal)
  - Gateway (all API traffic)
  - Policies: validate-jwt, rate-limit, rewrite-uri
     │
     ├──► [Function App — private endpoint]
     │       (HTTP backend)
     ├──► [Logic App Standard — private endpoint]
     │       (HTTP trigger backend)
     ├──► [Service Bus — send-request + MSI]
     │       (direct publish via REST API)
     └──► [On-prem via ExpressRoute / VPN]
             (Self-hosted gateway or VNET routing)

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  POLICY DOCUMENT STRUCTURE

  <policies>
    <inbound>
      <!-- Runs when request arrives at APIM -->
      <base />                          <!-- inherit parent scope policies -->
      <validate-jwt ... />
      <rate-limit-by-key ... />
      <set-header name="X-Correlation-Id" ... />
      <rewrite-uri template="/v2/orders/{orderId}" />
    </inbound>

    <backend>
      <!-- Controls APIM→backend call -->
      <base />
      <authentication-managed-identity resource="https://servicebus.azure.net" />
      <forward-request timeout="30" />
    </backend>

    <outbound>
      <!-- Runs after backend responds, before caller receives -->
      <base />
      <set-header name="X-Powered-By" exists-action="delete" />
      <json-to-xml ... />
    </outbound>

    <on-error>
      <!-- Runs on any exception -->
      <base />
      <set-header name="X-Error-Id" exists-action="override">
        <value>@(context.RequestId)</value>
      </set-header>
      <return-response>
        <set-status code="500" reason="Internal Server Error" />
        <set-body>{ "error": "@(context.LastError.Message)" }</set-body>
      </return-response>
    </on-error>
  </policies>

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  DIRECT SERVICE BUS PUBLISH FROM APIM (send-request + MSI)

  Client ──POST /orders──► APIM Gateway
                               │
                    [inbound policies]
                               │
                    [send-request to SB REST API]
                    POST https://{ns}.servicebus.windows.net/{queue}/messages
                    Authorization: Bearer {MSI token for servicebus.azure.net}
                               │
                    [return 202 to client, no backend forward]

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  PRODUCT / SUBSCRIPTION ACCESS MODEL

  Products
  ├── Internal Product (no approval, 1000 calls/min)
  │     ├── Orders API
  │     └── Inventory API
  └── Partner Product (approval required, 100 calls/min)
        └── Orders API (read-only operations only)

  Subscriptions → issued per Product → carry subscription key
  Callers → pass Ocp-Apim-Subscription-Key header

interview_qa.q: Explain the APIM policy document structure and describe when each section executes.
interview_qa.a: A policy document has four sections. Inbound executes first, when the request arrives at APIM before it's sent to the backend — this is where you authenticate (validate-jwt, check-header), authorize (validate subscription, check claims), rate limit, transform the request (rewrite-uri, set-header, set-body), and set context variables. Backend executes when APIM is about to call the backend — you configure retry policies, timeouts, and backend authentication here (authentication-managed-identity adds the MSI token). Outbound executes after the backend returns a response but before it's sent to the caller — use it to strip internal headers (remove Server, X-Powered-By), transform response format (json-to-xml), or enrich with additional data. On-error executes when any unhandled exception occurs in any prior section — use it to return a consistent error schema and log correlation IDs. The base element in each section inherits policies from the parent scope (global, product, API) — I always include it unless I intentionally want to override parent policies completely.

interview_qa.q: Walk me through the validate-jwt policy — what does it check and how do you configure it for Azure AD?
interview_qa.a: The validate-jwt policy validates a JWT Bearer token in the Authorization header. For Azure AD, you configure the openid-config-url pointing to the Azure AD tenant's OIDC discovery endpoint, which APIM uses to retrieve the signing keys automatically and rotate them as Azure AD updates them. You set required-claims to validate the audience (aud claim must match your API's app registration URI or client ID) and optionally the roles or scp claims. APIM checks: token signature (against keys from OIDC discovery), issuer (iss must match your tenant's issuer URL), expiry (exp), not-before (nbf), and any required-claims you configure. If validation fails, APIM returns 401 before the request reaches the backend. A common pattern I use is extracting a claim from the validated token into a context variable: <set-variable name='userId' value='@(context.Request.Headers["Authorization"].First().Substring(7).AsJwt()?.Subject)' /> and then forwarding it as a backend header so the backend knows who the caller is without re-validating the token.

interview_qa.q: How do you publish directly to Service Bus from APIM using Managed Identity? Walk through the policy.
interview_qa.a: This pattern eliminates the need for a Function App or Logic App as an intermediary when the only requirement is to enqueue a message. The policy uses authentication-managed-identity to get an MSI token for the Service Bus resource, then send-request to call the Service Bus REST API. First, assign the APIM managed identity the 'Azure Service Bus Data Sender' RBAC role on the Service Bus namespace. Then in the inbound policy: use authentication-managed-identity to acquire the token, store it in a variable, then use send-request to POST to https://{namespace}.servicebus.windows.net/{queue}/messages with the token in the Authorization header and the request body as the message payload. Finally, return a 202 Accepted to the caller without forwarding to any backend. I've used this pattern at EY for an event ingestion API where we needed to accept high volumes of events and queue them for async processing — the APIM→Service Bus path is more reliable and has fewer moving parts than APIM→Function→Service Bus.

interview_qa.q: What is the difference between Internal and External APIM mode, and how does the App Gateway + APIM pattern work?
interview_qa.a: External mode gives APIM a public IP — callers on the internet reach it directly. Internal mode deploys APIM inside a VNET with no public IP — it's only reachable from within the VNET or from connected private networks. The App Gateway + APIM pattern is the standard enterprise topology: Application Gateway (with WAF) has the public IP and faces the internet. It terminates TLS, applies OWASP WAF rules, and forwards traffic to APIM's private IP. APIM then applies API-level policies (JWT validation, rate limiting) and routes to private backends. The routing is controlled by App Gateway's backend pool pointing to APIM's private IP, with path-based rules if needed. DNS is the tricky part: APIM's developer portal and gateway have custom domain names (api.company.com). You configure these custom domains in APIM and point your public DNS to App Gateway's public IP. App Gateway uses host header-based routing to forward api.company.com traffic to the APIM gateway and portal.company.com to the developer portal. APIM must have the correct custom domain configured with certificates matching what App Gateway presents upstream.

interview_qa.q: How do you use Named Values and Key Vault references in APIM policies?
interview_qa.a: Named Values (formerly Properties) are a key-value store in APIM that externalize constants and secrets from policy XML. In policy XML you reference them with double curly syntax: {{my-named-value}}. They come in three types: plain (visible in portal), secret (encrypted, masked in portal), and Key Vault reference (points to a specific Key Vault secret version or the latest version). Key Vault references are the most secure option — APIM retrieves the secret value at runtime using its managed identity, and you rotate the secret in Key Vault without touching any policy XML. I configure APIM's managed identity with Key Vault Secrets User role on the Key Vault, then create a Named Value with type Key Vault pointing to the secret URI. When the policy executes, APIM resolves the named value to the current secret value. For backend API keys, OAuth client secrets, and SMTP credentials, I always use Key Vault references — it's also required for PCI and ISO27001 compliance at EY.

interview_qa.q: Describe the rate-limit-by-key policy and how you use it for fine-grained throttling.
interview_qa.a: rate-limit-by-key is more flexible than the basic rate-limit policy because you define the key — it can be the subscription key, the caller's IP, a claim from the JWT, or any context expression. The policy specifies calls (number of allowed calls), renewal-period (in seconds), and counter-key (the expression defining the throttling unit). For example, throttling by subscription: <rate-limit-by-key calls='100' renewal-period='60' counter-key='@(context.Subscription.Id)' />. For throttling by IP: counter-key='@(context.Request.IpAddress)'. For throttling per user based on a JWT claim: counter-key='@(context.Request.Headers["Authorization"].First().AsJwt()?.Subject)'. When the limit is exceeded, APIM returns 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header. I combine this with the quota policy (daily/weekly/monthly call limits) for product-level limits and rate-limit-by-key for per-session burst control. I put rate limiting in the inbound section before validate-jwt so that DDoS attempts are throttled before spending time on JWT validation.

interview_qa.q: How do you monitor APIM in production — what metrics and logs do you look at?
interview_qa.a: APIM integrates with Application Insights — you enable it at the APIM resource level and configure sampling rate. It emits request events as Application Insights requests (from the caller's perspective) and dependencies (APIM→backend calls). I build dashboards tracking: total requests, error rate by API and operation, average backend latency, p95 latency, and rate-limit-exceeded count by subscription. In Log Analytics I query the ApiManagementGatewayLogs table for detailed request traces including response codes, backend URLs, and policy execution time. I set up metric alerts for: backend error rate > 5% (triggers investigation), gateway error rate > 1% (triggers incident), and latency p95 > 2 seconds. For the Service Bus publish pattern I mentioned, I add custom dimensions using the emit-metric policy to track message publish failures separately from API errors. The built-in APIM Analytics blade in the portal gives a good overview but Log Analytics gives the flexibility to join with other data sources.

interview_qa.q: Explain the rewrite-uri policy and when you need it.
interview_qa.a: The rewrite-uri policy transforms the URL that APIM forwards to the backend, independently of the URL the client called. The most common use case is API versioning: externally you expose /v2/orders/{orderId} but the backend still expects the old /api/orders/{orderId}. You configure the APIM operation to match /v2/orders/{orderId} and add rewrite-uri with template '/api/orders/{orderId}'. Another use case is path normalization when aggregating multiple microservices behind a single APIM — each service has its own path structure and APIM presents a unified path to consumers. In the BizTalk migration context, we used rewrite-uri to expose clean RESTful URLs from APIM while the Logic App HTTP trigger URL included a path element APIM stripped. The policy is simple: <rewrite-uri template="/api/{orderId}" copy-unmatched-params="true" />. The copy-unmatched-params attribute controls whether query parameters not in the template are forwarded — usually true.

interview_qa.q: How do you handle backend failures in APIM — retry, circuit breaker, timeout?
interview_qa.a: APIM's backend policy section handles reliability. The retry policy wraps the forward-request and retries on specified HTTP status codes or exceptions: <retry condition='@(context.Response.StatusCode == 503)' count='3' interval='2' >. The forward-request has a timeout attribute controlling how long APIM waits for the backend before returning 504. For circuit breaker behavior, the newer APIM circuit-breaker policy (available in newer API versions) automatically opens the circuit when a backend exceeds an error threshold and routes to a fallback backend or returns a cached response. For backends defined as Backend resources in APIM (not inline URLs), you configure circuit breaker directly on the Backend object. I also use the Backend resource approach to centralize backend URL configuration — all policies reference the Backend by name rather than hardcoding URLs, so promoting a backend URL change from staging to prod is a one-place change. Load balancing across multiple backend instances uses the Backend Pool feature.

interview_qa.q: Describe the APIM + Function App + Service Bus integration pattern end to end.
interview_qa.a: The pattern: external client calls APIM (authenticated with subscription key and JWT). APIM's inbound policy validates the JWT, checks the subscription, applies rate limiting, sets a correlation ID header. APIM routes to the Function App backend (private endpoint, no public access). The Function App — triggered by HTTP — processes the request: validates business rules, transforms the payload, and publishes to Service Bus using the output binding with managed identity. The Function returns 202 Accepted with a correlation ID. APIM's outbound policy strips internal headers and returns 202 to the caller. Asynchronously, a Logic App workflow is triggered by the Service Bus message, orchestrates downstream processing (calls SAP, updates database), and on completion publishes a result to a response queue. If the caller needs the final result, they poll a status endpoint (also behind APIM, backed by another Function that reads from a Cosmos DB where the Logic App writes its completion status). This is the async request-reply pattern — the same pattern BizTalk used with its MessageBox for async orchestrations, now implemented with Azure-native services.

talking_points: At EY, APIM became our single front door for all integration APIs. Before APIM, each Logic App had its own HTTP trigger URL exposed to partners. Key rotation was a nightmare — each URL had its own SAS token, distributed to 12 different partner teams. With APIM, partners call a stable URL with a subscription key, and the Logic App URLs are internal secrets. We've rotated Logic App keys twice without any partner communication needed.

The send-request policy for direct Service Bus publish was something I introduced after we had a reliability incident. We had APIM→Function App→Service Bus and the Function App became a chokepoint during peak load. The Function App scaled, but cold starts added latency spikes. The direct APIM→Service Bus path via send-request removed the Function App entirely from the critical path for event ingestion. APIM acquired the MSI token, posted to the Service Bus REST API, and returned 202 in under 50ms consistently.

The App Gateway + Internal APIM topology was mandated by our security team — no APIM gateway IP should be publicly resolvable. Setting it up correctly required understanding the DNS split: public DNS for api.ey-integration.com points to App Gateway's public IP; App Gateway has a backend pool with APIM's private IP and overrides the host header; APIM has api.ey-integration.com configured as its custom domain with the certificate. Getting the certificate chain right between App Gateway and APIM was the tricky part — APIM validates the backend certificate when App Gateway connects to it on HTTPS.

Named Values with Key Vault references were a compliance requirement for us. Our security audit specifically asked how we managed API keys and OAuth secrets in APIM policies. I could point to the Key Vault reference configuration and show that no secrets were stored in APIM itself — just references. The APIM managed identity had Key Vault Secrets User role, secrets were versioned, and rotation was automated. That answer satisfied the auditor.

The validate-jwt policy is where I usually see misconfigurations in the field. The most common mistake is not pinning the audience claim — people configure the policy to check the issuer but forget to add a required-claims block for the audience. Without audience validation, any valid token from the same Azure AD tenant — including tokens for completely different applications — passes the policy. I always require both issuer and audience, and I add a required-claim for roles or scp to ensure the token has the right permission scope for the API.

code_snippets.label: APIM Policy — Full inbound: validate-jwt + rate-limit + correlation ID
code_snippets.code:
<policies>
  <inbound>
    <base />

    <!-- Rate limit before expensive JWT validation -->
    <rate-limit-by-key
      calls="100"
      renewal-period="60"
      counter-key="@(context.Request.IpAddress)"
      increment-condition="@(true)"
      remaining-calls-header-name="X-RateLimit-Remaining"
      retry-after-header-name="Retry-After" />

    <!-- Validate Azure AD JWT -->
    <validate-jwt
      header-name="Authorization"
      failed-validation-httpcode="401"
      failed-validation-error-message="Unauthorized: Invalid or missing token"
      require-expiration-time="true"
      require-scheme="Bearer">
      <openid-config url="https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant-id}/v2.0/.well-known/openid-configuration" />
      <required-claims>
        <claim name="aud">
          <value>api://my-api-app-registration-id</value>
        </claim>
        <claim name="roles" match="any">
          <value>Orders.Read</value>
          <value>Orders.Write</value>
        </claim>
      </required-claims>
    </validate-jwt>

    <!-- Extract caller identity from JWT for downstream use -->
    <set-variable
      name="callerId"
      value="@(context.Request.Headers["Authorization"].First().Substring(7).AsJwt()?.Subject)" />

    <!-- Correlation ID: use incoming or generate new -->
    <set-header name="X-Correlation-Id" exists-action="skip">
      <value>@(context.RequestId)</value>
    </set-header>
    <set-header name="X-Caller-Id" exists-action="override">
      <value>@((string)context.Variables["callerId"])</value>
    </set-header>

    <!-- Rewrite path for backend -->
    <rewrite-uri template="/api/v1/orders/{orderId}" copy-unmatched-params="true" />
  </inbound>

  <backend>
    <base />
    <!-- Authenticate to Function App backend using MSI -->
    <authentication-managed-identity resource="https://management.azure.com/" />
    <forward-request timeout="30" fail-on-error-status-code="true" />
  </backend>

  <outbound>
    <base />
    <!-- Strip internal headers from response -->
    <set-header name="X-Powered-By" exists-action="delete" />
    <set-header name="Server" exists-action="delete" />
    <!-- Add correlation ID to response -->
    <set-header name="X-Correlation-Id" exists-action="override">
      <value>@(context.RequestId)</value>
    </set-header>
  </outbound>

  <on-error>
    <base />
    <set-header name="X-Error-Correlation-Id" exists-action="override">
      <value>@(context.RequestId)</value>
    </set-header>
    <return-response>
      <set-status code="@(context.Response.StatusCode)" reason="@(context.Response.StatusReason)" />
      <set-header name="Content-Type" exists-action="override">
        <value>application/json</value>
      </set-header>
      <set-body>@{
        return new JObject(
          new JProperty("correlationId", context.RequestId),
          new JProperty("error", context.LastError?.Message ?? "Unexpected error"),
          new JProperty("statusCode", context.Response.StatusCode)
        ).ToString();
      }</set-body>
    </return-response>
  </on-error>
</policies>

code_snippets.label: APIM Policy — Direct Service Bus publish using Managed Identity (send-request)
code_snippets.code:
<policies>
  <inbound>
    <base />

    <!-- Validate JWT first -->
    <validate-jwt header-name="Authorization" failed-validation-httpcode="401">
      <openid-config url="https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant-id}/v2.0/.well-known/openid-configuration" />
      <required-claims>
        <claim name="aud"><value>api://event-ingestion-api</value></claim>
      </required-claims>
    </validate-jwt>

    <!-- Acquire MSI token for Service Bus -->
    <authentication-managed-identity
      resource="https://servicebus.azure.net"
      output-token-variable-name="sbToken" />

    <!-- Store request body in variable (body can only be read once) -->
    <set-variable name="requestBody" value="@(context.Request.Body.As<string>(preserveContent: true))" />

    <!-- Set correlation ID -->
    <set-variable name="correlationId" value="@(context.RequestId)" />

    <!-- Publish directly to Service Bus REST API -->
    <send-request mode="new" response-variable-name="sbResponse" timeout="10" ignore-error="false">
      <set-url>https://{{service-bus-namespace}}.servicebus.windows.net/{{queue-name}}/messages</set-url>
      <set-method>POST</set-method>
      <set-header name="Authorization" exists-action="override">
        <value>@("Bearer " + (string)context.Variables["sbToken"])</value>
      </set-header>
      <set-header name="Content-Type" exists-action="override">
        <value>application/json</value>
      </set-header>
      <set-header name="BrokerProperties" exists-action="override">
        <value>@{
          return new JObject(
            new JProperty("CorrelationId", (string)context.Variables["correlationId"]),
            new JProperty("MessageId", context.RequestId)
          ).ToString();
        }</value>
      </set-header>
      <set-body>@((string)context.Variables["requestBody"])</set-body>
    </send-request>

    <!-- Check Service Bus response -->
    <choose>
      <when condition="@(((IResponse)context.Variables["sbResponse"]).StatusCode != 201)">
        <return-response>
          <set-status code="502" reason="Bad Gateway" />
          <set-body>{ "error": "Failed to enqueue message", "correlationId": "@(context.RequestId)" }</set-body>
        </return-response>
      </when>
    </choose>

    <!-- Return 202 immediately — no backend forwarding -->
    <return-response>
      <set-status code="202" reason="Accepted" />
      <set-header name="Content-Type" exists-action="override">
        <value>application/json</value>
      </set-header>
      <set-body>{ "accepted": true, "correlationId": "@(context.RequestId)" }</set-body>
    </return-response>
  </inbound>

  <backend>
    <!-- Never reached — return-response in inbound short-circuits -->
    <forward-request />
  </backend>

  <outbound><base /></outbound>
  <on-error><base /></on-error>
</policies>

code_snippets.label: APIM Policy — Backend retry and circuit breaker
code_snippets.code:
<policies>
  <inbound><base /></inbound>

  <backend>
    <base />
    <retry
      condition="@(context.Response.StatusCode == 503 || context.Response.StatusCode == 429)"
      count="3"
      interval="2"
      max-interval="10"
      delta="1"
      first-fast-retry="false">
      <forward-request timeout="30" />
    </retry>
  </backend>

  <outbound>
    <base />
    <!-- Add retry count to response header for observability -->
    <set-header name="X-Retry-Count" exists-action="override">
      <value>@(context.Variables.ContainsKey("retryCount") ? context.Variables["retryCount"].ToString() : "0")</value>
    </set-header>
  </outbound>

  <on-error><base /></on-error>
</policies>

code_snippets.label: Bicep — APIM resource with managed identity and App Insights integration
